dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain. Location is the Greyhound bus station in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've had chest pain on the left side of my sternum since I found out my luggage was lost two hours ago.
Can you tell me the date today?
It is the 13th.
Where are we?
We are at the Greyhound bus station in Richmond.
What brought you to call 911?
Chest pain and some anxiety over lost luggage.
Have you experienced chest pain like this before?
Yes, I've had heart attacks before, but this pain doesn't feel as bad.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: What were you doing when the pain started?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. - events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I was waiting at the bus station and got a call about my lost luggage.
Are you having any other symptoms such as shortness of breath or dizziness?
Not really. Just chest pain.
What medications are you currently taking?
Latuda, Doxidan, Xanax, and Minipress.
Do you have any history of heart disease or mental health issues?
Yes, I've had heart attacks before and I'm diagnosed with PTSD, anxiety, and major depression.
Do you have any known drug allergies?
No.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate your chest pain?
It's about a 5 on the scale, not the worst I have had.
Your airway looks patent, and you are speaking clearly.
Yep, I can speak just fine.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Breathing is normal with no signs of labored effort.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Rate and depth of respirations
dialogue.utterance: Respiratory rate is 16 per minute and depth is adequate.

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Accessory Muscles Used with Breathing
dialogue.utterance: Do you feel you're using any extra effort when breathing?

dialogue.micro_intent: check breathing - Accessory Muscles Used with Breathing
dialogue.utterance: No. Only the chest pains are the issue.
Radial pulses are strong and regular bilaterally.
Skin is warm, dry, and without any visible abnormalities or injuries.
Pulse is 75 beats per minute.
Respiratory rate is 16 per minute.
Blood pressure is 168 over 95.
Glucose level is 123 mg/dL.
Oxygen saturation is at 99%.
4-lead EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: May I check your pupils now?

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: Yes.

dialogue.micro_intent: check circulation - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: Pupils are round, equal, and reactive.
Do you take any blood thinning medications?
No, I don't take blood thinners.
Were stents placed during your heart attacks?
No, there were no stents involved.
We're about to give you an aspirin to help reduce inflammation now.
Okay, let's do it.
Administering 324 mg of aspirin orally. Please chew it thoroughly and swallow.
Placing you on a 4-lead cardiac monitor.
The 4-lead is up, and the rhythm is normal sinus.
We will now obtain a 12-lead EKG for further assessment. Ready?
Yes.
The 12-lead EKG is placed. We see a left axis deviation but no signs of STEMI or other abnormalities.
I'll attempt to start an IV at a 20G in your left hand now.
I am unable to access a vein in the left hand at this time.
Based on our assessment, we'll follow Protocol 2-1 for suspected cardiac chest pain.
EKG remains normal with sinus rhythm.

dialogue.micro_intent: retake vital signs - ekg - left axis deviation
dialogue.utterance: 12-lead showing left axis deviation again with no changes.
Would you like to walk into the ambulance or prefer the stretcher?
I can walk, I don't need a stretcher.
We'll assist you in. You ambulated steadily earlier.
Please fasten your seatbelt. The five-point seat belt will help keep you safe during transport.
We can take you to Retreat Hospital Cold. Is that acceptable?
Retreat is fine for me.
Hospital contact has been made. Unit en route with a male with suspected non-cardiac chest pain.
Patient remained stable during the move with no acute changes observed.
Patient is alert and oriented to time, person, place, and event-AAOX4.
Vital signs, route, and symptoms stay consistent during transport.
GCS is 15 (E4, V5, M6). No distress observed during your movement inside.
No new symptoms reported. No signs of distress during transport.
Medication administration and vital signs monitoring are confirmed.
Cross-checking medications and procedures with partner for accuracy.
All checks out; no discrepancies.
Patient at the ED stood up and walked to the bed independently.
All vitals remained stable during transport. No incident reported.
Transfer of care and documentation have been completed with the receiving facility.
The patient was handed over to a registered nurse. No further EMS needed.
Are there any additional sounds or sensations while you're breathing?
No. I hear nothing abnormal.
Skin color is normal without any indicators of pallor or jaundice.

dialogue.micro_intent: head and neck exam - Pupillary reflex / Size of pupils
dialogue.utterance: Pupils continue to be equal and reactive to light at this point.

dialogue.micro_intent: primary assessment check breathing - Flaring of nostrils
dialogue.utterance: No flaring of nostrils. Breathing remains non-labored.

dialogue.micro_intent: body position - Preferred posture to maintain airway
dialogue.utterance: You seem to be breathing most comfortably in an upright seated position.
Rather than IV access, I'm proceeding with direct contact and monitoring until we stabilize your transport to the hospital.

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Can you move your arms and legs right now?

dialogue.micro_intent: extremities and back exam - Gross motor and sensory function
dialogue.utterance: Yes, I can move them easily.
No evident deformity or unusual angulation noted.
Visible minor scars on the back, likely from past surgical or accidental history, but currently not relevant.
We noted earlier efforts to use arms more for breathing but it appears to be minimal for you now.
No change in accessory muscle use or breathing effort detected during the last check.
Attempted an IV start in a vein on your left hand using a 20G, but we couldn't secure access at this time.
If needed, we'll revisit IV access or consider alternative routes upon arrival at the ED.
Following suspected cardiac protocol, we'll continue monitoring closely.
Transporting you safely in a position of comfort, which you have chosen independently today.
I'll be maintaining constant monitoring of your condition en route.
Rest assured, communication to the hospital has been made, and we'll be arriving shortly.
We'll check vitals again soon if we detect any changes during our transport.
What time do you think it is right now?
I believe it is after 4 in the morning.
That's correct. You're oriented to time, which is great to see.
We'll still repeat a quick PsA check in a bit to verify that things remain stable.
During reassessment, I'll make sure to prompt for any change in symptoms or breathing effort.
EKG tracing is still normal with no new or worsening findings during your transport thus far.
Would you mind if I do a quick breath sound check again just to be thorough?
Go ahead, I'm fine with that.
I've heard no change in or new breath sounds. You're breathing well as the transport continues.
The environment is cold today, but warm blankets or other methods are on hand in case you are uncomfortable.
I'm okay, no need for blankets.
Just providing the assurance that it's safe and that we have options if needed.
I just want to highlight, from my assessment, you've shown no signs of worsening condition since we've engaged.
Our next step is to continue communication with the hospital on your status upon arrival.
Our remaining actions will be minimal unless we detect any new findings.
It's absolutely normal if you are a bit anxious given the situation. You're in good hands.
Understand that IV at 20G in the left hand will be attempted again if needed upon arrival.
We're doing all that we can here in pre-hospital to ensure safety and optimal communication.
Thank you. I appreciate your help.
Any time. That's what we're here for.
